coredata.dc:title: Cross-linkage induced fluorescence: Fabrication of fluorescent organic particles via reaction between polyethyleneimine and dopamine-containing copolymers
coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Xin
Liu, Meiying
Yi, Xiaoqing
Long, Wei
Tian, Jianwen
Chen, Lihua
Zhu, Weifeng
Li, Xiancai
Zhang, Xiaoyong
Wei, Yen
coredata.dc:description: Dopamine is the most abundant catecholamine neurotransmitter in the brain and has extensive applications in biomedicine and materials science. Here, novel metal-free fluorescent organic particles (FOPs) with excellent biocompatibility and high photostability, namely DA-PEG-PEI, were successfully synthesized through combination of ring-opening reaction and crosslinking reaction between polyethyleneimine (PEI) and dopamine-containing copolymers (DA-PEGDGE). Because of the crosslink-enhanced emission (CEE) effect of PEI and a quantity of functional groups such as catechol and amines on the surface of the particles, DA-PEG-PEI FOPs possessed high water dispersibility and superior optical properties, making them highly potential for biological imaging applications. Moreover, owing to the existence of functional components/groups, such as amines, catechol groups, the resultant DA-PEG-PEI FOPs can be potentially applied for loading or carrying different bioactive molecules, such as anticancer agent bortezomib, siRNA and CPG oligodeoxynucleotide through formation of dynamic bonds or electrostatic interaction. Furthermore, this reaction can occur under ambient environment, room temperature, in the present of oxygen and without catalysts. Therefore, we believe that this work could not only provide a simple and elegant route for preparation of FOPs, but also have great potential for construction of multifunctional theranostic platforms.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent organic particles
Cross-linkage induced fluorescence
Dopamine-containing copolymers

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Confocal microscopic imaging of cells using DA-PEG-PEI FOPs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of DA-PEG-PEI FOPs and intermediate DA-PEGDGE 1H NMR.
XPS spectra of DA-PEGDGE (black line) DA-PEG-PEI (red line). (A) Survey scan of XPS spectra; (B) C 1s region; (C) N 1s region; (D) O 1s region.
TEM images of DA-PEG-PEI FOPs with different magnifications, scale bar = 0.5 μm (A), and 200 nm (B), respectively.

(A) Images of water dispersion of DA-PEGDGE, DA-PEG-PEI particles (concentration of both is 0.625 mg mL−1) under 365 nm UV irradiation; (B) The excitation and emission spectra of DA-PEG-PEI FOPs in water; (C) FL spectra of DA-PEG-PEI FOPs under different excitation wavelengths (320–420 nm); (D) FL spectra of the DA-PEG-PEI FOPs in different pH (2-12) solutions were used to determine the effect of pH on the fluorescence properties.

Viability of HeLa cells after incubation with DA-PEG-PEI FOPs at different concentrations (6.25–200 μg mL−1) for 24 h. The cell evaluation results suggested that DA-PEG-PEI FOPs showed negative toxicity to cells.
Confocal lasing scanning microscopy (CLSM) images of HeLa cells incubated with 40 μg mL−1 of DA-PEG-PEI FOPs, the scale bar is 20 μm.
Schematic illustration of the preparation of DA-PEG-PEI FOPs by crosslinking the DA-containing polymer (DA-PEGDGE) with PEI.

head.ce:title: Cross-linkage induced fluorescence: Fabrication of fluorescent organic particles via reaction between polyethyleneimine and dopamine-containing copolymers

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Nanchang University, 999 Xuefu Avenue, Nanchang, 330031, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Nanchang University, 999 Xuefu Avenue, Nanchang 330031, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry and the Tsinghua Center for Frontier Polymer Research, Tsinghua University, Beijing, 100084, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and the Tsinghua Center for Frontier Polymer Research

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry and the Tsinghua Center for Frontier Polymer Research, Tsinghua University, Beijing, 100084, P. R. China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Dopamine is the most abundant catecholamine neurotransmitter in the brain and has extensive applications in biomedicine and materials science. Here, novel metal-free fluorescent organic particles (FOPs) with excellent biocompatibility and high photostability, namely DA-PEG-PEI, were successfully synthesized through combination of ring-opening reaction and crosslinking reaction between polyethyleneimine (PEI) and dopamine-containing copolymers (DA-PEGDGE). Because of the crosslink-enhanced emission (CEE) effect of PEI and a quantity of functional groups such as catechol and amines on the surface of the particles, DA-PEG-PEI FOPs possessed high water dispersibility and superior optical properties, making them highly potential for biological imaging applications. Moreover, owing to the existence of functional components/groups, such as amines, catechol groups, the resultant DA-PEG-PEI FOPs can be potentially applied for loading or carrying different bioactive molecules, such as anticancer agent bortezomib, siRNA and CPG oligodeoxynucleotide through formation of dynamic bonds or electrostatic interaction. Furthermore, this reaction can occur under ambient environment, room temperature, in the present of oxygen and without catalysts. Therefore, we believe that this work could not only provide a simple and elegant route for preparation of FOPs, but also have great potential for construction of multifunctional theranostic platforms.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Since the first report about the mussel-inspired chemistry for surface modification was published [1], numerous research interests and efforts have been focused on this field because of its simplicity, versatility and broad applicability in materials science [2,3]. Dopamine (DA), the most abundant catecholamine neurotransmitter in the brain [4], has been demonstrated the main reason of the strong adhesive capacity of mussels onto marine substrates. Inspired by the adhesion mechanism, dopamine has played an essential role in preparing various smart sticky materials [5,6], photosensitizers [7,8], antibacterial materials [9], chemotherapy drug delivery system [10,11], and fluorescent probes [12] on account of its superb biocompatibility and unique physicochemical properties. Specifically, its intrinsic functional groups such as primary amine and catechol moieties endow DA with the ability to interact with almost all inorganic/organic/metallic surfaces through covalent bonds or non-covalent bonds [1,13]. For example, Yang et al. developed a tunable biomimetic NO-generating coating (DA-CuII) by dip-coating method in which an aqueous solution containing DA and copper ions (CuII) was used [14]. The coated cardiovascular stents exhibited outstanding hemocompatibility and high NO catalytic efficacy. Feng et al. fabricated a novel fluorescent probe, anthrancene-dopamine thioether, which showed excellent sensitive and selective recognition to Hg2+ when there are other important related metal ions and amino acid interferences at the same time in HEPES solution. The catechol groups contribute mainly to coordinate with Hg2+ and chelation-enhanced fluorescence enhancement [15]. Generally, the interaction between polymers can be strengthened if material itself has catechol structure.

In the past decade, developing heavy metal-free, photostable and biocompatible photoluminescent particles for cell imaging, fluorescent molecular probes, sensors, gene delivery carries and so on has gained considerable attention in biomedical fields. Fluorescent materials such as traditional fluorescent proteins, fluorescent inorganic nanoparticles (FINs) and fluorescent organic particles (FOPs) have been the key materials in sensing, biomedical imaging, diagnosis, and entertainment industries [16–19]. Traditional fluorescent proteins have been widely explored for bio-imaging applications on account of their good fluorescence performance, excellent biocompatibility and biodegradability. Notwithstanding, most of them often suffer from some intrinsic drawbacks, such as high cost, low membrane permeability and infamous aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) effect [20]. FINs comprising quantum dots [21–23], up conversion nanoparticles, silicon-based nanomaterials, noble metal nanoclusters and so on have been extensively investigated for various potential applications including the light-emitting diodes (LEDs) [24] and biological labels [25] for their special performance in optics [26], preparation [27] and cost. Nevertheless, their major drawbacks, potential cytotoxicity, and poor degradability, cannot be ignored that have impeded their practical biomedical applications. Therefore, nontoxic and high fluorescence particles are desirable in biomedical imaging and early diagnosis of disease [28].

In addition to the superiority of good biocompatibility, FOPs are superior to FINs and fluorescent proteins in more variability and flexibility in materials synthesis [29,30]. Li et al. designed a new fluorescence “off-on” analysis for directly furfural detection based on pH-sensitive poly-(DOPA) FOPs, which are produced by a “one-pot” in-situ polymerization approach [31]. This fluorescent detection method is responsive and precise. Zhang et al. prepared three red FOPs in high yield through hydrophobic−hydrophilic interactions between cyano-substituted diarylethlene derivatives and Pluronic F127, and investigated their potential biomedical applications [32]. Nevertheless, the FOPs were restricted due to the complex and multi-step synthetic route, poor dispersion and high cost, etc., for further exploration [33]. Hence, it is in high demand to develop an eco-friendly, high-efficient, and economical method in preparation of the particles. Polyethylenimine (PEI) is a water-soluble cationic polymer embracing a number of active amine groups on both the backbone and branched chains [34]. As a polymer containing primary (25%), secondary (50%), tertiary (25%) amines at the same time, branched PEI can coordinate with metal atoms tightly, and form strong hydrogen bonds with organic compounds incorporating O- or N- functional groups. In these cases, the long-chain PEI has attracted extensive attention in drug delivery [35], biosensor [36], water treatment [37], pesticide residue analysis [38], and so on. It has been proven that PEI has the ability to enhance the fluorescence degree and photostability of carbon dots and polymers. Zhang et al. reported an approach of preparing of PDA based FOPs via self-polymerization of dopamine and cross-linkage by PEI (M w = 600 Da) in 2015 [38]. Li et al. has successfully synthesized a novel crosslinked PEI particles which consist of numerous carbon dots, namely CDs@PEI, and evaluated its biological application [39]. With the help of branched PEI, the hydrophilicity, fluorescence performance and biocompatibility of the materials have been greatly enhanced [40]. Therefore, PEI based FOPs can be expected to have a bright prospect of the biomedicine application.

In this contribution, we reported a novel strategy for construction of FOPs which were synthesized via a two-step procedure. Firstly, the precursor, catechol-functionalized copolymer DA-PEGDGE, was prepared via the ring-opening reaction between DA and poly (ethylene glycol) diglycidyl ether. Then, PEI was reacted with DA-PEGDGE to obtain the crosslinked DA-PEG-PEI FOPs. As illustrated in Scheme 1 , the FOPs were formed under mild experimental conditions, including ambient temperature, in aqueous solution and catalyst-free. Through the above simple preparation procedure, DA-PEG-PEI FOPs with strong green fluorescence, well water dispersibility, and excellent biocompatibility can be fabricated and were potentially utilized for biological imaging and drug delivery and immunotherapy.

All reagents were of analytical grade or better. Poly (ethylene glycol) diglycidyl ether (PEGDGE, Mn ≈ 500 g/mol), Dopamine hydrochloride (DA·HCl), Polyethyleneimine (PEI) (Mw = 600 Da), Triethylamine (TEA), Tris-HCl, Hydrochloric acid (HCl, 37%) and 1-methyl-2-pyrrolidinone (NMP) were purchased from Aladdin Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China) and used as received.

DA-PEGDGE was synthesized through ring-cleavage reaction according to the literature [41]. Briefly, DA·HCl (1 mmol, 190 mg) and PEGDGE (1 mmol, 500 mg) were dissolved in NMP (5 mL) under N2 atmosphere at ambient temperature and the pH of the solution was adjusted to the alkaline-stage (pH ≈ 10) using TEA. The pH of the reaction was adjusted to 4–5 after 48 h. Then the viscous polymer DA-PEGDGE was obtained by dialyzed with distilled water for 24 h and lyophilization.

DA-PEG-PEI FOPs was prepared through the crosslinking DA-PEGDGE with PEI. 100 mg DA-PEGDGE and 100 mg PEI were dissolved in Tris-HCl buffer (pH = 7.4) and reacted for 12 h at room temperature. Afterwards, the mixture was dialyzed successively against distilled water and absolute ethanol for 24 h through a dialysis bag (molecular weight cutoff 1000 Da). Finally, the resulting products was carried out by vacuum drying at 45 °C overnight.

The physical, chemical, optical properties of the DA-PEG-PEI FOPs were characterized by 1H and 13C NMR spectra, Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra, UV–vis and fluorescence spectra, transmission electron microscope (TEM), Dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurements, X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS). The detailed experimental apparatus is listed in supporting information.

The cell viability was confirmed via the cell counting kit-8 (CCK-8) assay to evaluate the potential biomedical application of DA-PEG-PEI FOPs [42]. The HeLa cells were used as the model cell line. The samples were incubated with the cells for 24 h and the concentration of DA-PEG-PEI FOPs was start from 6.25 to 200 μg mL−1. Detailed experimental procedures are elaborated in the supporting information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Confocal microscopic imaging of cells using DA-PEG-PEI FOPs
ce:section.ce:para: The cell uptake behavior of DA-PEG-PEI composites was explored with the help of confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM) and the concentration used for the imaging test is 40 μg mL−1. The test method of CLSM is detailed in supporting information.

DA-PEG-PEI FOPs were synthesized by the combination of ring-opening polymerization of dopamine with PEGDGE and crosslinking with PEI. Based on epoxy chemistry, epoxy and amine can undergo ring-opening reactions at room temperature, and 1H NMR spectroscopy was conducted to verified its structure as displayed. The distinct peak settled at 3.61 ppm should attribute to methene -O–CH2–CH2- of PEGDGE, and the weak signal at 3.90 ppm can be assigned to the proton of -CHC2-OH group in DA-PEGDGE which is the product of ring cleavage reaction of PEGDGE (Fig. 1 ). Meanwhile, a broad peak between 6.40 ppm and 6.90 ppm represents the presence of a benzene ring in catechol which confirmed the success synthesis of DA-PEGDGE. In the 13C NMR spectrum of DA-PEGDGE (Fig. S1), the signal peak of the carbon atoms on the skeleton of PEGDGE (-O-CH2 CH2–O-) is clearly located at 70.45 ppm. Meanwhile, the peaks at 45.56 ppm and 43.82 ppm should belong to -N-CH 2 -CH 2 -Ar on DA. PEI plays an important role in biomedicine and has numerous amines on its backbone from the perspective of structure. Both the characteristic signal of PEI (2.50–2.80 ppm) and PEGDGE (3.62 ppm) were clearly detected in Fig. 1 (red line), indicating that the PEI has conjugated with DA-PEGDGE polymer through crosslinking. Therefore, the 1H NMR spectra could demonstrate the successful synthesis of DA-based FOPs. FT-IR spectra were also utilized to verify its chemical structure as shown in supporting information (Fig. S2). The characteristic peaks of –CH2- stretching vibration (PEGDGE, 2882 cm−1), secondary amine and hydroxyl stretching (3333 cm−1), -C-N- and C–O stretching vibration (PEI, 1089 cm−1) and so on were all observed, implying the successful preparation of the FOPs.

The successful synthesis of DA-PEG-PEI FOPs has been further verified by XPS via detecting detailed chemical compositions in samples. As seen in Fig. 2 A, the major elements (C, N, O) of the samples were all observed. As shown in Fig. 2A, the peak intensity of N was obviously increased as compared with the samples of DA-PEGDGE and DA-PEG-PEI, indicating that PEI with high N content has reacted with DA-PEGDGE. Fig. 2B shows the detailed XPS spectra of C 1s. It can be clearly seen that the binding energies of C 1s of DA-PEGDGE is majorly located at 288 eV, which could be assigned to the CO originated from the oxidized phenol of DA. On the other hand, a shoulder peak located at about 286.5 eV was also seen in the spectrum of DA-PEGDGE. This shoulder peak is possibly ascribed to the C–O of PEGDGE. However, in the C 1s spectrum of DA-PEG-PEI, the binding energies were shifted to about 285 eV, indicating that a lot of C–N bonds were introduced in the sample DA-PEG-PEI. It is therefore, we could speculate that PEI with abundant C–N bonds was successfully conjugated with DA-PEGDGE through Michael addition reaction and Schiff base reaction. Fig. 2C is the N 1s spectra of DA-PEGDGE and DA-PEG-PEI. It can be seen that the peak intensity of N 1s is very weak in the spectrum of DA-PEGDGE. The existence of N in DA-PEGDGE implied PEGDGE reacted with DA through ring-opening reaction. The remarkable increase of peak intensity of N 1s suggests that PEI with high content of N was incorporated in DA-PEG-PEI successfully. The obvious change of N content also confirms that DA-PEG-PEI FOPs were prepared by crosslinking of PEI with DA-PEGDGE. Correspondingly, we also found that the oxygen content was significantly decreased by comparison of O 1s spectra of DA-PEG-PEI FOPs and DA-PEGDGE. This is because no oxygen was contained in PEI. Therefore, based on the results from survey scan and high resolution XPS spectra, we can conclude that we have prepared the fluorescent FOPs via the simple procedure in this work. Detailed information of the chemical compositions of the two samples were listed in Table S1, the percentages of C, N and O for DA-PEGDGE are 72.58%, 1.470% and 25.95%, respectively. The existence of element N suggested that DA has reacted with PEGDGE successfully. The element contents of C, N and O were changed to 86.17%, 7.270% and 6.560%, respectively, indicating the effectively preparation of the DA-PEG-PEI FOPs. The obvious increase of N and decrease of O indicated that PEI has successfully reacted with DA-PEGDGE via Michael addition reaction and Schiff base reaction. In addition, GPC test also applied to characterize the polymers. The molecular weight of DA-PEGDGE and DA-PEG-PEI is 6848 and 8202 Da with polydispersity index of 2.22 and 1.93, respectively (Table S2).

The structure and morphology of prepared DA-PEG-PEI FOPs was characterized by TEM. As shown in Fig. 3 , a distinct difference between the representative core-shell polymer particles and the synthesized FOPs can be found that the particles are not standard spherical with rough edge and uneven in size. The possible reason is the lack of distinct hydrophobic components in DA-PEG-PEI FOPs and the formation of particles is majorly relied on the cross-linkage of PEI and DA-PEGDGE. Moreover, the size of particles is obviously less than 500 nm and most of them are smaller than 200 nm based on the TEM observation. To further explore the particle diameter in aqueous solution, hydrodynamic size distribution of the DA-PEG-PEI FOPs in aqueous solution was measured through DLS (Fig. S3). Its average diameter is 505 ± 90 nm with a polydispersity index (PDI) of 0.808. This difference perhaps because the micelles have a tendency to collapse and shrink upon drying.

By virtue of the interaction between PEI and DA-PEGDGE, the FOPs displayed high dispersity in aqueous solution. As shown in the illustration of Fig. S4, the transmittance is relatively high and the background image could be observed clearly. Furthermore, no precipitation was observed after the suspensions of DA-PEG-PEI was left for a week (Fig. S5), indicating the high-water stability and great dispersibility of FOPs. In order to study their optical property, UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy was implemented on the above solution. In Fig. S4, there is an obvious absorption peak at 304 nm, which could be attributed to the n-π* transition of the PEI crosslinking with catechol, and a weak peak at 250 nm was also detected, which can be assigned to the π-π* transition of the aromatic ring in DA-PEG-PEI system, indicating the successful synthesis and self-assembly of DA-PEG-PEI copolymer in aqueous solution. In the usual sense, nonconjugated polymers without typical conjugated fluorophore groups should have no strong photoluminescence (PL). However, a new type of fluorescent composite with significant enhancing fluorescence emission only possessing sub-fluorophores have been prepared via chemical crosslinking or physical immobilization of polymer chains, which is named the crosslink-enhanced emission (CEE) effect. Owning to the CEE effect of PEI, strong fluorescence can be observed after PEI crosslinking with DA-PEGDGE, giving the prepared particles superior optical properties which may find potential application in biomedical field. The fluorescence property of DA-PEGDGE and DA-PEG-PEI polymers was investigated under UV radiation. Fig. 4 A presents that there is almost no fluorescence from the solution of the preformed DA-PEGDGE particles under 365 nm UV irradiation, while crosslinking product DA-PEG-PEI exhibited a bright green color. The fluorescence quantum efficiency of DA-PEG-PEI FOPs is determined to be 4.2%. Fluorescence spectroscopy was applied to distinguish the PL properties of DA-PEG-PEI FOPs in detail. The maximum emission wavelength was sited at 520 nm and the optimal excitation wavelength was located at 379 nm in Fig. 4B. In addition, the fluorescence spectra of both DA-PEGDGE and DA-PEG-PEI in the same concentration (0.625 mg mL−1) has supplemented in Fig. S6. As shown in Fig. S6, the fluorescence intensity of DA-PEG-DA is much stronger than that of DA-PEGDGE under the same concentration, which is a good proof of the existence of CEE effect. As presented in Fig. 4C, it is found that the emission peak dose not vary with the excitation wavelength. When the FOPs were excited with different wavelengths ranging from 320 nm to 420 nm, the emission peak was centered at 520 nm while the strongest fluorescence signal was obtained under 379 nm excitation wavelength. What's more, the effect of pH on the DA-PEG-PEI FOPs was also studied. The result in Fig. 4D confirmed that the above particles are stable in a wide pH range (2-12). Furthermore, under pH = 7 which is very close to the physiological environment, the FOPs have relatively maximum fluorescence intensity, implying their biomedical potential.

Therefore, the particles possess synergistic effects of reactive groups on DA and CEE effect of PEI, giving the DA-PEG-PEI FOPs distinctive optical properties such as strong fluorescence in aqueous solution, excitation-independent emission phenomenon, and stable fluorescence in a wide pH range, which are obviously different from carbon dots (CDs) and polydopamine FOPs [43,44]. Generally, the emission wavelength of CDs is sensitive to the excitation wavelength. Nevertheless, from the above experimental results, the emission wavelength of crosslinked PEI particles is independent of the excitation wavelength. Given the excellent water dispersibility and fluorescence properties of DA-PEG-PEI FOPs, the biocompatibility of DA-PEG-PEI FOPs was evaluated through CCK-8 assay using HeLa cells. As illustrated in Fig. 5 , there is nearly no significant decrease in cell viability (all above 95%) after incubated with 6.25–200 μg mL−1 of DA-PEG-PEI FOPs for 24 h, demonstrating their excellent biocompatibility. In addition, comparing with FINs, DA-PEG-PEI FOPs is more desirable in biological applications because of their unique fluorescence characteristic, excellent biocompatibility, easy operation and high water dispersibility.

The above biological results give evidence of that the crosslinked PEI particles are intrinsic FOPs with great promise for image-guided diagnosis. Thus, the cell uptake behavior of DA-PEG-PEI composites was detailed analyzed after HeLa cells incubated with the particles (40 μg mL−1) for 3 h. Intense fluorescence signal can be clearly observed in the cytoplasm of cells; however, relatively weak fluorescent signal was found in cell nucleus (Fig. 6) . The particles can hardly enter the cell nucleus mainly attributed to the size of the FOPs is rather larger than that of the nucleus pores. It can be also noticed that the cell morphology maintained very well during the test, confirming the excellent biocompatibility of DA-PEG-PEI FOPs, suggesting their great potential for bioimaging application. Moreover, the DA-PEG-PEI FOPs should also be promising for other biomedical applications to carry the anionic drugs or nuclear acids for intracellular delivery these bioactive molecules for the binding capability of PEI with them [45–47]. On the other hand, it is well known that catechol groups could form dynamic bonds with boronic acids, aldehyde groups or metal ions and these dynamic bonds can be pH responsiveness [48–53]. Therefore, the resultant DA-PEG-PEI FOPs are potentially utilized for construction of pH responsive drug delivery systems. We believe that this work will provide a novel idea and elegant route to fabricate multifunctional theranostic platforms.

In summary, a novel and simple strategy for fabrication of FOPs has been developed in this work, that was relied on the ring-opening reaction between the amino group of DA and epoxy group of PEGDGE and subsequent Michael addition reaction and Schiff base reaction between amino groups of PEI and DA of DA-PEGDGE. The DA-PEG-PEI FOPs showed outstanding water solubility on account of numerous functional groups (e.g., –NH, NH2 and –OH) on their surface. On the one hand, due to the intrinsic characteristic of DA and those active groups, the resultant FOPs can be applied to fabricate drug, gene delivery systems, targeting agents or other multifunctional theranostic platforms. On the other hand, thanks to the CEE effect of PEI, the particles possess excellent fluorescence characteristic and photostability, implying their capacity of biological imaging. Taking advantage of high-efficiency synthetic strategy, strong fluorescence, great dispersibility and excellent biocompatibility, the DA-PEG-PEI FOPs should have broad application prospects for fluorescence imaging, controlled intracellular drug delivery and immunotherapy. As compared with the traditional strategies for fabrication of FOPs, this work purposed a novel and elegant route for preparation FOPs with desirable optical and biological features.

Xin Hu: Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Meiying Liu: Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Xiaoqing Yi: Project administration, Funding acquisition, Resources, Data curation. Wei Long: Project administration, Funding acquisition, Resources, Data curation. Jianwen Tian: Project administration, Funding acquisition, Resources, Data curation. Lihua Chen: Project administration, Funding acquisition, Resources, Data curation. Weifeng Zhu: Project administration, Funding acquisition, Resources, Data curation. Xiancai Li: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Xiaoyong Zhang: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Yen Wei: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 81860405, 218650165, 21788102, 22265019, 22261035).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.dyepig.2023.111307.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Mussel-inspired surface chemistry for multifunctional coatings

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Lee H, Dellatore SM, Miller WM, Messersmith PB. Mussel-Inspired Surface Chemistry for Multifunctional Coatings. Science. 2007;318(5849):426-430.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Dopamine: just the right medicine for membranes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Yang H-C, Waldman RZ, Wu M-B, Hou J, Chen L, Darling SB, et al. Dopamine: Just the Right Medicine for Membranes. Adv Funct Mater. 2018;28(8):1705327.
Yang P, Zhu F, Zhang Z, Cheng Y, Wang Z, Li Y. Stimuli-responsive polydopamine-based smart materials. Chem Soc Rev. 2021;50(14):8319-8343.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Dopamine receptors: from structure to function

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: MISSALE C, NASH SR, ROBINSON SW, JABER M, CARON MG. Dopamine Receptors: From Structure to Function. Physiol Rev. 1998;78(1):189-225.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Construction of injectable silk fibroin/polydopamine hydrogel for treatment of spinal cord injury

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Chen S, Liu S, Zhang L, Han Q, Liu H, Shen J, et al. Construction of injectable silk fibroin/polydopamine hydrogel for treatment of spinal cord injury. Chem Eng J. 2020;399:125795.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Mussel-mimetic tissue adhesive for fetal membrane repair: an ex vivo evaluation

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Haller CM, Buerzle W, Kivelio A, Perrini M, Brubaker CE, Gubeli RJ, et al. Mussel-mimetic tissue adhesive for fetal membrane repair: An ex vivo evaluation. Acta Biomater. 2012;8(12):4365-4370.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Covalently assembled dopamine nanoparticle as an intrinsic photosensitizer and pH-responsive nanocarrier for potential application in anticancer therapy

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Li H, Zhao Y, Jia Y, Qu C, Li J. Covalently assembled dopamine nanoparticle as an intrinsic photosensitizer and pH-responsive nanocarrier for potential application in anticancer therapy. Chemical Communications. 2019;55(100):15057-15060.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Dopamine–quantum dot conjugate: a new kind of photosensitizers for photodynamic therapy of cancers

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Chou K-L, Meng H, Cen Y, Li L, Chen J-Y. Dopamine-quantum dot conjugate: a new kind of photosensitizers for photodynamic therapy of cancers. J Nanopart Res. 2012;15(1):1348.
Fu Y, Yang L, Zhang J, Hu J, Duan G, Liu X, et al. Polydopamine antibacterial materials. Mater Horiz. 2021;8(6):1618-1633.
